_comment: DatumIngest model catalog — v1 manifest read by the in-app Model Manager
and (eventually) the catalog-aware variants of scripts/download-*.ps1.
Schema notes:
- `licenses` (top-level) defines each license once. Per-model `licenseIds`
references entries here. The app uses `textFile` to render acceptance UI
offline; `canonicalUrl` is the authoritative source for verification.
- `requiresAcceptance` is a property of the LICENSE (does the user have to
click 'I accept' on this license text?). MIT/Apache = false. OpenRAIL-M
and similar = true.
- `requiresHfLogin` is a property of the MODEL (is the HF source repo
gated by HuggingFace, requiring a logged-in token to download?). This is
independent of the license — Llama is both gated AND has app-acceptance.
- `attributions` lists copyright holders / contributors that the upstream
license requires us to credit. Order is primary author first, then
derivative contributors (fine-tuner / quantizer / ONNX exporter).
Surfaced in the model card; HF READMEs inherit from this list.
Entries marked `placeholder: true` point at huggingface.co/Heliosoph/*
repos that do not exist yet — see models/upload-plan.json for the upload
plan that brings them online. The downloader should treat placeholder
entries as unavailable until they're cleared.
Revision pinning: v1 manifest uses `revision: 'main'` for simplicity. When
v2 ships, each model will be pinned to a specific commit sha so the catalog
is reproducible across HF history.
Verification: downloader queries HF's tree API at the specified revision,
which returns lfs.sha256 per file. The manifest does NOT bake per-file
hashes — they come from HF at download time.
Multi-source download: every entry declares `sources` as an ordered list.
The downloader tries each in order; the first source that can list and
deliver every expected file wins. Each source has a `type` discriminator
(today: 'huggingface', 'github-release', 'https') that selects which
client knows how to fetch from it. Heliosoph-mirror first is the policy:
pinned-sha Heliosoph entries lead for reproducibility; upstream URLs
trail as fallbacks (where present) so a Heliosoph outage doesn't block
downloads. Today most entries declare a single Heliosoph source — adding
an upstream fallback per entry is a future per-model judgement call.
Install SQL: a model entry may optionally include `installSql`, a path
(relative to models/) pointing at a SQL file that contains the canonical
`CREATE MODEL` statement(s) for the downloaded weights. After the
downloader places files on disk, it reads this SQL file and executes it
via the catalog so `models.<name>(...)` is callable from any query. The
file may contain multiple CREATE MODEL statements for multi-endpoint
bundles (CLIP image+text encoders, Whisper encoder+decoder, MobileSAM
image encoder + mask decoders). Entries without `installSql` simply
download the weights — registration is the user's responsibility (LLMs
via the built-in IModel path, SD bundles awaiting multi-session `infer()`).

mit.title: MIT License
mit.summary: Permissive open-source license. Allows commercial use, modification, and redistribution. Only requires preserving the original copyright notice.

apache-2.0.title: Apache License 2.0
apache-2.0.summary: Permissive open-source license with an explicit patent grant. Allows commercial use, modification, and redistribution. Requires preserving copyright/notice/attribution.

cc-by-4.0.title: Creative Commons Attribution 4.0 International
cc-by-4.0.summary: Permissive license. Allows commercial use, modification, and redistribution. Requires attribution to the original author.

bsd-2-clause.title: BSD 2-Clause License
bsd-2-clause.summary: Permissive open-source license, also known as the 'Simplified BSD' or 'FreeBSD' license. Allows commercial use, modification, and redistribution. Requires preserving copyright/notice in source + binary redistributions. The simpler sibling of BSD-3-Clause — drops the non-endorsement clause, so derivative products may use the upstream author's name without separate permission.

bsd-3-clause.title: BSD 3-Clause License
bsd-3-clause.summary: Permissive open-source license. Allows commercial use, modification, and redistribution. Requires preserving copyright/notice and forbids using the upstream author's name to endorse derivative products without permission.

creativeml-openrail-m.title: CreativeML OpenRAIL-M License
creativeml-openrail-m.summary: Open license with use-based restrictions. Commercial use OK. Prohibits CSAM, discrimination, automated legal/medical decision-making, misinformation generation, and similar misuse. Used by SD 1.x and its fine-tunes.

creativeml-openrail-pp-m.title: CreativeML OpenRAIL++-M License
creativeml-openrail-pp-m.summary: Stability AI variant of OpenRAIL-M for SDXL. Same use-based restrictions as OpenRAIL-M with tightened attribution clauses. Used by SDXL base and SDXL fine-tunes.

stability-ai-community.title: Stability AI Community License
stability-ai-community.summary: Source-available license from Stability AI. Free for research, individual use, and commercial use BELOW $1M annual revenue (above requires a Stability AI Enterprise License). Requires attribution and propagation of Stability's Acceptable Use Policy. Used by SD Turbo, SDXL Turbo, Stable Diffusion 3, and Stable Cascade.

llama-3.1-community.title: Llama 3.1 Community License
llama-3.1-community.summary: Source-available license from Meta. Allows commercial use BELOW 700 million monthly active users (above requires a separate Meta license). Requires 'Built with Llama' attribution and prefixing derivative model names with 'Llama'. Subject to Meta's Acceptable Use Policy.

falcon-llm-license-2.0.title: TII Falcon-LLM License 2.0
falcon-llm-license-2.0.summary: Custom permissive license from Technology Innovation Institute (TII), UAE. Allows commercial use, modification, and redistribution with attribution. Subject to TII's acceptable-use policy (no weapons / surveillance / defamation / CSAM use).

models.summary: Turn sentences into vectors so you can find similar ones with cosine similarity. The fastest, smallest English embedder — start here for semantic search and clustering.
models.description: Tiny, fast sentence embeddings (384-dim). Good default for similarity search.
attributions: sentence-transformers — Reimers & Gurevych (UKP Lab, TU Darmstadt)

models.summary: MiniLM-size English embedder with materially better retrieval accuracy. Drop-in replacement for MiniLM when you can spare ~50% more disk.
models.description: MTEB-leaderboard-grade English embeddings at MiniLM size.
attributions: BAAI — Beijing Academy of Artificial Intelligence

models.summary: English embedder that handles 8,000-token chunks (≈10 typical web pages at once). Use when you want to retrieve whole documents instead of slicing them into paragraphs first.
models.description: 8K-context English embeddings, 768-dim. Good for whole-document retrieval.

models.summary: Re-orders a list of candidate passages by how well they answer a query. Use as the second pass after a vector-similarity search to put the best match at the top.
models.description: Cross-encoder reranker — much more accurate than cosine similarity for top-k retrieval.
attributions: BAAI — Beijing Academy of Artificial Intelligence

models.summary: Tells whether a sentence sounds positive, negative, or neutral. Trained on tweets but works on any short English text — reviews, comments, support tickets.
models.description: 3-class sentiment (positive / negative / neutral). Generalizes beyond Twitter.

models.summary: Scores English text on six kinds of toxicity (toxic, severe, obscene, threat, insult, identity-hate). Use for moderating user-generated content.
models.description: 6-label toxicity (toxic / severe / obscene / threat / insult / identity-hate).

models.summary: One image model that does many things: write a caption, find objects, read text, or answer questions about the picture. Pick what it does per call via a task prompt. Half-precision GPU build.
models.description: Microsoft's unified vision-language model — captioning, OCR, detection, grounded VQA via task prompts.

models.summary: Same all-in-one image model as Florence-2 fp16 — captioning, OCR, detection, VQA from task prompts — half the disk, runs comfortably on CPU.
models.description: INT8-quantized Florence-2 for CPU/NPU/mobile. ~half the size of fp16 with modest OCR-quality cost.

models.summary: Describe an image in one sentence. Classic baseline captioner — fast and adequate; reach for Florence-2 if you also need OCR or object detection from the same model.
models.description: Classic ViT encoder + GPT-2 decoder. Vanilla 'describe this image' baseline.

models.summary: Tiny (1.9B) image-aware chat model with Apache-2.0 licensing throughout. Captions images and answers questions about them. Pick when permissive license and small footprint matter more than top-tier quality.
models.description: 1.86B-param vision-language model from Vikhyat Korrapati. Surprisingly capable for its size — does captioning, VQA, and basic grounding. Apache-2.0 throughout. Drive via raw onnxruntime sessions (the onnxruntime-genai model_builder doesn't currently accept Moondream2's architecture).
attributions: Heliosoph (Optimum ONNX export from vikhyatk/moondream2 PyTorch weights)

models.summary: Find and box common objects in a photo (people, cars, dogs — 80 COCO categories). Recommended starting point for object detection.
models.description: Apache-2.0 alternative to YOLOv8. 80 COCO classes. Recommended general default.
attributions: Megvii Inc. — YOLOX (Ge, Liu, Wang, Li, Sun)

models.summary: Smallest YOLOX — fits on phones and edge devices (~4 MB). Use when latency and disk matter more than peak accuracy.
models.description: Smallest YOLOX (0.9M params). For mobile/edge/CPU detection.
attributions: Megvii Inc. — YOLOX (Ge, Liu, Wang, Li, Sun)

models.summary: Biggest YOLOX — highest accuracy in the family. Pick when GPU is available and you want the fewest false positives.
models.description: Largest YOLOX (99M params). For GPU + accuracy-critical detection.
attributions: Megvii Inc. — YOLOX (Ge, Liu, Wang, Li, Sun)

models.summary: Step up from YOLOX-Nano with meaningfully better accuracy at minimal extra cost. The pragmatic pick for edge devices that can spare a few MB.
models.description: 5.1M params. Step up from Nano with materially better mAP at minimal extra cost.
attributions: Megvii Inc. — YOLOX (Ge, Liu, Wang, Li, Sun)

models.summary: Mid-size YOLOX. Runs comfortably on CPU; flies on GPU. The default 'I want quality without a big model' pick.
models.description: 25.3M params. Balanced speed/accuracy. CPU-viable, GPU-faster.
attributions: Megvii Inc. — YOLOX (Ge, Liu, Wang, Li, Sun)

models.summary: Larger YOLOX — sharper accuracy than M on small / crowded objects. Plan for GPU.
models.description: 54.2M params. Higher accuracy than M; GPU recommended.
attributions: Megvii Inc. — YOLOX (Ge, Liu, Wang, Li, Sun)

models.summary: Original YOLOX backbone — included for reproducibility. The CSPDarknet variants (M / L) generally beat it on accuracy-per-cost; pick this only when you need this specific architecture for comparison.
models.description: 63.7M params. Original Darknet backbone variant; included for reproducibility. CSPDarknet variants (M/L) generally supersede this on accuracy/cost.
attributions: Megvii Inc. — YOLOX (Ge, Liu, Wang, Li, Sun)

models.summary: Modern transformer-based object detector — same 80 COCO categories as YOLOX but skips the non-maximum-suppression post-processing step. Cleaner output pipeline, slightly different speed profile.
models.description: Baidu's real-time DEtection TRansformer with ResNet-18 backbone. 80 COCO classes, ~46 mAP, no NMS post-processing.
attributions: Baidu Inc. — RT-DETR (Lv, Xu, Zhao et al., PaddlePaddle team)

models.summary: Half-precision RT-DETR-R18 — same detector, half the disk. Pick on GPUs with native fp16 for a small speedup at no quality cost.
models.description: Half-precision RT-DETR-R18. Same architecture, ~half the disk. Quality near-identical to fp32 on GPUs with native fp16; on CPU runtimes that upcast, expect no quality change but no speed win.
attributions: Baidu Inc. — RT-DETR (Lv, Xu, Zhao et al., PaddlePaddle team)

models.summary: Aggressively quantized RT-DETR-R18 — ~quarter the disk, CPU-friendly. Modest accuracy drop on small / crowded scenes; best when deployment size matters.
models.description: INT8 dynamic-quantized RT-DETR-R18. ~quarter the disk of fp32 and CPU-friendly on most runtimes (ORT, OpenVINO NPU). Modest mAP drop vs fp32 on small/crowded scenes.
attributions: Baidu Inc. — RT-DETR (Lv, Xu, Zhao et al., PaddlePaddle team)

models.summary: Click or draw a box on something in an image and get a clean cutout mask of it. The phone-sized cousin of Meta's Segment Anything — works from point or box prompts.
models.description: MobileSAM ViT-T encoder + SAM mask decoders (single + multi-mask). Interactive segmentation from clicks, boxes, or mask hints. ~60 MB total.
attributions: Meta AI Research — Segment Anything (Kirillov, Mintun, Ravi et al.)
Chaoning Zhang et al. — MobileSAM (Kyung Hee University)

models.summary: Cut the main subject of a photo out of its background. Lightweight default (~5 MB) — use for product shots, profile pictures, anywhere you want a transparent background.
models.description: Distilled U²-Net for binary salient-object (foreground) segmentation. 4.7M params, ~4.7 MB, 320×320 input. Output is a single-channel mask separating subject from background — pair with image_cutout() for background removal. Recommended default; full u2net is only worth the disk hit on tricky fine-edge cases (hair, fur).
attributions: Xuebin Qin, Zichen Zhang, Chenyang Huang, Masood Dehghan, Osmar R. Zaiane, Martin Jagersand — U²-Net (University of Alberta)

models.summary: Full-size salient-object cutout. Same job as the Lite variant but with cleaner edges around hair, fur, lace, and other thin structures.
models.description: Full U²-Net (176M params, ~170 MB, 320×320 input). Same task as u2netp but with sharper edges on hair / fur / lace / thin structures. Pick this over u2netp only when fine-detail boundaries matter and disk + latency aren't constrained.
attributions: Xuebin Qin, Zichen Zhang, Chenyang Huang, Masood Dehghan, Osmar R. Zaiane, Martin Jagersand — U²-Net (University of Alberta)

models.summary: Guess how far away each pixel of a photo is. High-quality relative depth (no absolute scale) — pick on GPU when accuracy on fine details matters.
models.description: Intel ISL Dense Prediction Transformer for monocular depth estimation. 384×384 input. Outputs relative depth (not metric).
attributions: Intel ISL — Ranftl, Bochkovskiy, Koltun (DPT / MiDaS)

models.summary: Lightweight relative depth from a single image — ~50 ms per image on consumer CPU. Use when latency and disk matter more than absolute quality.
models.description: Lightweight EfficientNet-Lite3 + depth-decoder monocular depth estimator. ~21M params, 256×256 BGR input. ~50ms per image on consumer CPU. Sibling to DPT-Large but ~16× smaller — pick this when latency / disk matter more than absolute quality.
attributions: Intel ISL — Ranftl, Lasinger, Hafner, Schindler, Koltun (MiDaS)

models.summary: Sharper relative-depth estimator with a stronger backbone than DPT / MiDaS. Recommended modern default for monocular depth — best quality-per-MB in this catalog.
models.description: Current SOTA permissive-license general-purpose monocular depth. DINOv2 ViT-S encoder + DPT-style decoder, ~24M params. Faster than DPT-Large, often sharper boundaries. Relative depth (not metric). Recommended modern default — best quality-per-MB in this catalog.
attributions: Lihe Yang, Bingyi Kang, Zilong Huang, Zhen Zhao, Xiaogang Xu, Jiashi Feng, Hengshuang Zhao — Depth Anything V2 (TikTok / HKU)

models.summary: Mid-size Depth Anything V2 — better detail on fine boundaries than the Small variant. Pick on CPU when accuracy beats throughput.
models.description: Mid-size variant of Depth Anything V2 — DINOv2 ViT-B encoder, ~97M params. Quality close to the V2-Large variant without V2-Large's CC-BY-NC-4.0 license issue (V2-Large is non-commercial; V2-Base is Apache-2.0). Pick over the Small variant when accuracy on fine detail matters more than CPU throughput.
attributions: Lihe Yang, Bingyi Kang, Zilong Huang, Zhen Zhao, Xiaogang Xu, Jiashi Feng, Hengshuang Zhao — Depth Anything V2 (TikTok / HKU)

models.summary: Latest generation Depth Anything — best monocular relative depth quality in this catalog. Bonus: also predicts camera pose alongside depth, closer to a unified geometric-understanding model than a depth-only network.
models.description: Latest Depth Anything generation. Successor to V2 from a partially-new team — large DINOv2 backbone, ~1.4 GB total (external-data ONNX). Headline change: **Apache-2.0 across all variants including Large** (V2-Large was CC-BY-NC, kept it out of the catalog). Additionally produces **camera pose estimation** as a secondary output, not just depth — closer to a unified geometric-understanding model than a depth-only network. Should give visibly smoother surfaces than V2-Base on the surface-noise issues that show up in the depth+Poisson pipeline.
attributions: Haotong Lin, Sili Chen, Jun Hao Liew, Donny Y. Chen, Zhenyu Li, Guang Shi, Jiashi Feng, Bingyi Kang — Depth Anything V3

models.summary: Lightweight Metric3D V2 — ViT-Small backbone, CPU-friendly. Pick for edge / latency-bound use, or as a quick first-pass before a heavier model.
models.description: Smallest variant of Metric3D V2 — ViT-Small backbone (~22M params, ~100 MB on disk). Same outputs and task contracts as Large (metric depth in meters + per-pixel surface normals), but trained at a much smaller capacity. Quality is meaningfully behind Large on hard outdoor scenes, but for CPU inference or a quick triage pass over many frames, it's an order of magnitude faster. Useful as the cheap-and-fast tier in a quality-routed photogrammetry pipeline (Small for triage, Large for keepers, Giant for finals).
attributions: Mu Hu, Wei Yin, Chi Zhang, Zhipeng Cai, Xiaoxiao Long, Hao Chen, Kaixuan Wang, Gang Yu, Chunhua Shen, Shaojie Shen — Metric3D V2 (HKU + Shanghai AI Lab)

models.displayName: Metric3D V2 Small fp16 (Metric Depth + Normals)
models.summary: Lightweight Metric3D V2 — ViT-Small backbone, CPU-friendly. Pick for edge / latency-bound use, or as a quick first-pass before a heavier model. ~Half the disk and model-load memory, same outdoor-strong metric depth + surface normals outputs.
models.description: Smallest variant of Metric3D V2 — ViT-Small backbone (~22M params, ~100 MB on disk). Same outputs and task contracts as Large (metric depth in meters + per-pixel surface normals), but trained at a much smaller capacity. Quality is meaningfully behind Large on hard outdoor scenes, but for CPU inference or a quick triage pass over many frames, it's an order of magnitude faster. Useful as the cheap-and-fast tier in a quality-routed photogrammetry pipeline (Small for triage, Large for keepers, Giant for finals).
attributions: Mu Hu, Wei Yin, Chi Zhang, Zhipeng Cai, Xiaoxiao Long, Hao Chen, Kaixuan Wang, Gang Yu, Chunhua Shen, Shaojie Shen — Metric3D V2 (HKU + Shanghai AI Lab)

models.summary: Outputs **real-world metric depth** and **surface normals** from a single image in one forward pass. Specialty: outdoor scenes (parking lots, landscapes, mixed distances) where indoor-trained metric depth models fall off.
models.description: Cross-camera-generalizing metric depth model. Versatile monocular geometric foundation model — outputs metric depth AND surface normals from a single forward pass. Trained on a much larger and more diverse outdoor corpus than ZoeDepth's NYU+KITTI, with a metric-normalization scheme that adapts to arbitrary camera intrinsics. Strong on outdoor scenes where DAv3 / ZoeDepth's training distribution falls off (parking lots, landscapes, mixed-distance shots). Pair with DAv3 for indoor work + Metric3D for outdoor in a domain-routed photogrammetry pipeline.
attributions: Mu Hu, Wei Yin, Chi Zhang, Zhipeng Cai, Xiaoxiao Long, Hao Chen, Kaixuan Wang, Gang Yu, Chunhua Shen, Shaojie Shen — Metric3D V2 (HKU + Shanghai AI Lab)

models.displayName: Metric3D V2 Large fp16 (Metric Depth + Normals)
models.summary: Half-precision build of Metric3D V2 Large — ~half the disk and model-load memory, same outdoor-strong metric depth + surface normals outputs.
models.description: Half-precision variant of the Large model — ~825 MB on disk vs ~1.6 GB for fp32. Same architecture, same outputs (metric depth in meters + per-pixel surface normals), same outdoor-friendly training distribution. On GPU / NPU with native fp16 expect a modest throughput win over fp32; on CPU runtimes that upcast fp16 → fp32 internally, latency matches fp32 but the model-load memory cost is roughly halved. Pick this variant when container size or VRAM footprint matters more than the negligible numerical-precision difference.
attributions: Mu Hu, Wei Yin, Chi Zhang, Zhipeng Cai, Xiaoxiao Long, Hao Chen, Kaixuan Wang, Gang Yu, Chunhua Shen, Shaojie Shen — Metric3D V2 (HKU + Shanghai AI Lab)

models.summary: Largest Metric3D V2 variant — ViT-Giant backbone (~1B params, ~5.5 GB). Best quality on hard outdoor scenes; GPU required.
models.description: Largest variant of Metric3D V2 — ViT-Giant backbone (~1B params, ~5.5 GB on disk). Best absolute accuracy in the family, especially on long-range outdoor scenes, mixed-distance compositions, and edge-case geometry where the smaller variants degrade. Needs ~6 GB of VRAM and ~8 GB of system RAM for usable inference latency; CPU inference is technically possible but painfully slow. Pick when accuracy genuinely matters and the GPU budget allows — for production batch photogrammetry rather than interactive workflows.
attributions: Mu Hu, Wei Yin, Chi Zhang, Zhipeng Cai, Xiaoxiao Long, Hao Chen, Kaixuan Wang, Gang Yu, Chunhua Shen, Shaojie Shen — Metric3D V2 (HKU + Shanghai AI Lab)

models.displayName: Metric3D V2 Giant fp16 (Metric Depth + Normals)
models.summary: Half-precision build of Metric3D V2 Giant — ~half the disk and model-load memory, same outdoor-strong metric depth + surface normals outputs.
models.description: Half-precision variant of the Giant model — ~2.7 GB on disk vs ~5.5 GB for fp32. Same architecture, same outputs (metric depth in meters + per-pixel surface normals), same outdoor-friendly training distribution. On GPU / NPU with native fp16 expect a modest throughput win over fp32; on CPU runtimes that upcast fp16 → fp32 internally, latency matches fp32 but the model-load memory cost is roughly halved. Pick this variant when container size or VRAM footprint matters more than the negligible numerical-precision difference.
attributions: Mu Hu, Wei Yin, Chi Zhang, Zhipeng Cai, Xiaoxiao Long, Hao Chen, Kaixuan Wang, Gang Yu, Chunhua Shen, Shaojie Shen — Metric3D V2 (HKU + Shanghai AI Lab)

models.summary: Monocular depth in **real-world meters** (not just relative). Use when you need consistent scale across photos for 3D reconstruction, distance measurement, or AR overlays.
models.description: **Metric** depth estimation — outputs real-world distances in meters, not just relative depth. DPT-Large backbone + dual metric heads (NYU indoor + KITTI outdoor calibration). Use when absolute scale matters: 3D point-cloud reconstruction at consistent scale across images, distance measurement, AR-style overlays. Heavier than the relative-depth models (~1.3 GB); for a smaller deployment footprint use the fp16 sibling entry.
attributions: Shariq Farooq Bhat, Reiner Birkl, Diana Wofk, Peter Wonka, Matthias Müller — ZoeDepth (Intel ISL)
Heliosoph (Optimum / torch.onnx.export from Intel/zoedepth-nyu-kitti PyTorch weights)

models.summary: Same real-world-meter depth as ZoeDepth NYU+KITTI, half the disk. Pick for tighter deployment footprint with no quality change on most CPUs.
models.description: Half-precision variant of ZoeDepth (NYU+KITTI). ~660 MB on disk vs ~1.3 GB for fp32 — same metric depth output. Pick when deployment footprint matters (container images, edge bundles); on GPU/NPU with native fp16, expect a modest speedup. On CPU runtimes that upcast fp16→fp32 internally, expect identical latency to fp32 but half the model-load memory.
attributions: Shariq Farooq Bhat, Reiner Birkl, Diana Wofk, Peter Wonka, Matthias Müller — ZoeDepth (Intel ISL)
Heliosoph (Optimum + onnxconverter-common fp16 conversion from Intel/zoedepth-nyu-kitti PyTorch weights)

models.summary: Turn a single photo of one object into a 3D mesh you can rotate, render, or 3D-print. The model hallucinates the back side the camera couldn't see. Object-scale (one subject centered in frame), not whole-scene.
models.description: Single-image to 3D mesh in one shot. DINOv2 ViT image encoder + triplane decoder + implicit field, all trained jointly so the model can hallucinate the back / occluded sides of an object from a single front-facing photo — the complement to the depth+Poisson pipeline (which only captures what the camera sees). Object-scale, not scene-scale: works best on a single subject centered in frame. Outputs a watertight triangle mesh ready for browser rendering or 3D printing.
attributions: Stability AI + Tripo AI / VAST-AI-Research — TripoSR (Tochilkin, Pankratz, Liu, Huang, Letts, Li, Liang, Laforte, Jampani, Cao, 2024)
Heliosoph (ONNX export from upstream PyTorch weights)

models.summary: Lightweight monocular depth from a different model family than DPT / Depth Anything. Mostly interesting for side-by-side comparison demos; the modern Depth Anything V2 Small beats it on quality.
models.description: Global-Local Path Network for monocular depth from KAIST (2022). EfficientFormer encoder + custom decoder, ~52M params. Older than Depth Anything V2 but solid as a lightweight baseline — useful as an architectural-diversity row in a comparison demo (different backbone family from the DPT / Depth Anything DINOv2 lineage).
attributions: Doyeon Kim, Woonghyun Ka, Pyungwhan Ahn, Donggyu Joo, Sehwan Chun, Junmo Kim — GLPN (KAIST)

models.summary: Find faces in a photo, with eye / nose / mouth keypoints per face. Tiny (~3 MB), Apache-2.0 throughout. Use as the first stage of any face workflow.
models.description: BlazeFace-derived face detector (256×256, ~896 SSD anchors) emitting box + 6 BlazeFace keypoints (eyes, nose, mouth, tragions) + confidence per face. Ships alongside the 468-point MediaPipe FaceMesh landmark model (currently not wired to a SQL surface — bundled for future two-stage workflows). Tiny (~3 MB combined). Apache-2.0 throughout.

models.summary: Same face detector as MediaPipe Face, quantized to half the size. Slight accuracy drop on small / distant faces — use on CPU / NPU / mobile.
models.description: INT8-quantized MediaPipe Face detector + FaceMesh bundle. ~half the disk of the float variant. CPU / NPU / mobile target. Modest accuracy drop on small/distant faces.
attributions: Qualcomm AI Hub (ONNX export + W8A8 quantization)

models.summary: Find text regions in an image (no recognition). Tiny (~5 MB). Pair with TrOCR or Florence-2 to read what's inside the boxes.
models.description: Text-region detector for OCR pipelines. Tiny (~5 MB). Outputs polygon coordinates per text region. Pair with TrOCR or Florence-2 for end-to-end OCR.

models.summary: Read printed text from a single cropped line image. Pair with PaddleOCR detection (or Florence-2) for end-to-end document OCR.
models.description: Microsoft's Transformer-based OCR — ViT image encoder + GPT-style text decoder, trained on printed-text crops (SROIE dataset). Recognizes the text in a cropped image of a single line/word. Pair with a text-region detector (PaddleOCR or Florence-2 detection) for end-to-end document OCR. Encoder + decoder + tokenizer travel together.
attributions: Microsoft Research — TrOCR (Li, Lv, Yu, Cui, Lu, Wei, 2021)
Heliosoph (Optimum ONNX export from microsoft/trocr-base-printed PyTorch weights)

models.summary: Same printed-text recognizer as TrOCR Base Printed, half the disk. Pick when deployment footprint matters.
models.description: Half-precision TrOCR Base Printed — ~half the disk of the fp32 variant. Same encoder-decoder architecture, same printed-text training. Pick this when deployment footprint matters (containerized inference, edge bundles); on GPU / NPU with native fp16, expect a modest speedup. On CPU runtimes that upcast fp16 → fp32, expect identical latency to fp32 but half the model-load memory.
attributions: Microsoft Research — TrOCR (Li, Lv, Yu, Cui, Lu, Wei, 2021)
Heliosoph (Optimum fp16 ONNX export from microsoft/trocr-base-printed PyTorch weights)

models.summary: Upscale a photo by 4× while cleaning up real-world artifacts (JPEG compression, sensor noise, mild blur). Tiny (~5 MB) but works well on real photos and screenshots.
models.description: General-purpose 4x image super-resolution. Tiny (~5 MB) but punches above its weight on real photos and screenshots. Tile large inputs to manage memory.

models.summary: Higher-quality 4× upscaler than Real-ESRGAN x4v3 on the model's pinned 64×64 tiles. Also restores real-world degradations during the upscale. Plan for GPU.
models.description: Swin Transformer for Image Restoration — real-world super-resolution variant. Upscales by 4× while cleaning up real-world degradations (compression artifacts, sensor noise, mild blur). SwinIR-L config, 240-dim embeddings. Pinned 64×64 input → 256×256 output; tile larger inputs.
attributions: Jingyun Liang, Jiezhang Cao, Guolei Sun, Kai Zhang, Luc Van Gool, Radu Timofte — SwinIR (ETH Zurich + others)

models.summary: Removes moderate-noise color noise (the σ=25 benchmark level). Pick when you have known synthetic-noise conditions; for real-world phone-camera noise prefer SCUNet Color Real PSNR.
models.description: SwinIR color denoising variant trained for Gaussian noise σ=25. SwinIR-M config, 180-dim embeddings. Pinned 128×128 input. Use for the standard denoising-benchmark σ=25 reference; for blind real-world noise pick scunet-color-real instead.
attributions: Jingyun Liang, Jiezhang Cao, Guolei Sun, Kai Zhang, Luc Van Gool, Radu Timofte — SwinIR (ETH Zurich + others)

models.summary: Real-world color photo denoiser. Handles unknown sensor + JPEG + compression artifacts without needing the noise level. Recommended default for general photo cleanup.
models.description: Blind real-world color denoising — handles unknown noise patterns (sensor + JPEG + downsampling artifacts) without needing the noise level. PSNR-optimized: stays faithful to the input, no GAN sharpening. Recommended default for general-purpose photo denoising. Dynamic H/W (must be divisible by 8).
attributions: Kai Zhang, Yawei Li, Jingyun Liang, Jiezhang Cao, Yulun Zhang, Hao Tang, Deng-Ping Fan, Radu Timofte, Luc Van Gool — SCUNet

models.summary: Same real-world color denoiser, sharper and more 'photographic' output. Invents plausible texture (hair, fabric). Pick for consumer cleanup; avoid when fidelity matters.
models.description: Same blind real-world denoiser as the PSNR variant, trained with adversarial loss. Output is sharper and more 'photographic' — invents plausible texture (hair, fabric) the noisy input couldn't pin down. Pick for consumer photo cleanup; avoid when fidelity matters.
attributions: Kai Zhang, Yawei Li, Jingyun Liang, Jiezhang Cao, Yulun Zhang, Hao Tang, Deng-Ping Fan, Radu Timofte, Luc Van Gool — SCUNet

models.summary: Color Gaussian-noise denoiser tuned for light noise (σ=15 — ISO-grain territory). Use for matched-condition denoising or benchmark reproduction.
models.description: Color Gaussian-noise specialist trained at σ=15 (light noise — ISO-grain territory). Beats the blind variant when input noise actually matches σ≈15; over-smooths cleaner inputs. Use for matched-condition denoising or benchmark reproduction.

models.summary: Color Gaussian-noise denoiser tuned for moderate noise (σ=25 — typical night-photo level). The standard benchmark reference.
models.description: Color Gaussian-noise specialist trained at σ=25 (moderate noise — typical night phone photo). The standard denoising-benchmark reference level. Use for σ≈25 matched conditions or apples-to-apples comparison with other published denoisers at σ=25.

models.summary: Color Gaussian-noise denoiser tuned for heavy noise (σ=50 — extreme low-light). Aggressive enough to flatten heavy noise; over-smooths clean inputs.
models.description: Color Gaussian-noise specialist trained at σ=50 (heavy noise — extreme low-light, old digital cameras). Aggressive enough to flatten heavy noise; over-smooths on cleaner inputs. Use for σ≈50 matched conditions or visualizing wrong-specialist artifacts in a comparison demo.

models.summary: Single-channel grayscale denoiser at σ=15. ~3× faster than running color SCUNet on duplicated channels. For medical, document, and scientific imagery.
models.description: Grayscale (single-channel) Gaussian denoising at σ=15. ~3× faster than running the color variant on replicated channels. Use for medical/document/scientific grayscale denoising at matched σ≈15 conditions.

models.summary: Single-channel grayscale denoiser at σ=25. Standard reference for grayscale denoising — chest X-rays, scanned documents, B&W photos.
models.description: Grayscale Gaussian denoising at σ=25. Single-channel input, single-channel output. The standard benchmark reference for grayscale denoising. Pair with chest X-ray / document / B&W photo workflows.

models.summary: Single-channel grayscale denoiser at σ=50. For heavily-noisy grayscale workflows — low-light astrophotography, degraded scans.
models.description: Grayscale Gaussian denoising at σ=50 (heavy noise). Single-channel input/output. Use for heavily-noisy grayscale workflows (low-light astrophotography, degraded scans) at matched σ≈50 conditions.

models.summary: Label what's in a photo with one of 1,000 ImageNet categories (e.g. 'tabby cat', 'sports car'). Tiny CPU-friendly baseline.
models.description: Tiny 1000-class ImageNet image classifier (~14 MB). CPU-friendly baseline for whole-image labelling, embedding extraction, or as a backbone for fine-tuning. Bundles the ImageNet-1K class label file.
attributions: Google AI Research — MobileNetV2 (Sandler, Howard, Zhu, Zhmoginov, Chen)

models.summary: Turn text into spoken audio in eleven bundled voices (American + British, male + female). Tiny (~320 MB) and surprisingly natural for its size.
models.description: 82M-param TTS with 11 bundled voices (American + British, male + female). Punches above its weight.

models.summary: Generate expressive speech — including laughs, sighs, and embedded sound effects — from text. Runs as a Python worker. Pick the small variant for affordable GPU inference.
models.description: Suno's compact Bark TTS variant (~100M params). Generates expressive speech with embedded sound effects via a Python worker; weights download into the configured models directory like every other model.

models.summary: Full Suno Bark TTS — more natural speech than Bark Small at 3–4× the inference cost. Use when expressiveness matters more than throughput.
models.description: Full Suno Bark TTS (~700M params). More natural speech than Bark Small at ~3-4× the inference cost; same Python worker, same dependency set.

models.summary: Generate photoreal-leaning images from text prompts in just 4 sampling steps. SD 1.5-based all-purpose workhorse, SFW.
models.description: SFW general-purpose SD 1.5 fine-tune + 4-step distillation. Photorealistic-ish workhorse.
attributions: ByteDance — Hyper-SD LoRA (Ren, Xia, Zhang et al.)
CompVis / Stability AI — Stable Diffusion 1.5 base

models.summary: Generate stylized fantasy, painterly, or illustrated images from text prompts in 4 steps. Best for D&D-style art and concept illustration.
models.description: Stylized SFW SD 1.5 fine-tune — fantasy, painterly, illustrative. Good for D&D-style art.
attributions: ByteDance — Hyper-SD LoRA (Ren, Xia, Zhang et al.)
CompVis / Stability AI — Stable Diffusion 1.5 base

models.summary: Generate photoreal environments — landscapes, architecture, interiors — in 4 steps. Pick when the subject is 'a place' rather than 'a person'.
models.description: Photorealistic environments + natural lighting. Pick over Realistic Vision when the subject is 'a place'.
attributions: ByteDance — Hyper-SD LoRA (Ren, Xia, Zhang et al.)
CompVis / Stability AI — Stable Diffusion 1.5 base

models.summary: Generate images in modern Disney / Pixar style — the model expects the activator phrase `modern disney style` somewhere in your prompt. 4 sampling steps.
models.description: Modern Disney/Pixar style fine-tune. Activator: 'modern disney style'.
attributions: ByteDance — Hyper-SD LoRA (Ren, Xia, Zhang et al.)
CompVis / Stability AI — Stable Diffusion 1.5 base

models.summary: Generate images in a Midjourney-v4 look — activator phrase is `mdjrny-v4 style`. 4 sampling steps.
models.description: Midjourney-v4-style fine-tune. Activator: 'mdjrny-v4 style'.
attributions: ByteDance — Hyper-SD LoRA (Ren, Xia, Zhang et al.)
CompVis / Stability AI — Stable Diffusion 1.5 base

models.summary: Generate photoreal portraits — sharper faces and skin texture than the other Hyper variants. More NSFW-permissive base.
models.description: Photorealistic portraits. More NSFW-permissive than the other Hyper variants.
attributions: ByteDance — Hyper-SD LoRA (Ren, Xia, Zhang et al.)
CompVis / Stability AI — Stable Diffusion 1.5 base

models.summary: High-resolution (1024×1024) text-to-image — SDXL quality in 4 sampling steps. Photoreal and fantasy from a single fine-tune. Plan for GPU + ~8 GB VRAM.
models.description: SDXL fine-tune + Lightning distillation. 1024×1024, 4 steps, photorealistic + fantasy.
attributions: RunDiffusion — JuggernautXL Lightning (SDXL fine-tune + Lightning distillation)
Stability AI — Stable Diffusion XL base
ByteDance — SDXL Lightning method (Lin, Wang, Yang)

models.summary: Stability AI's distilled SD 2.1 — 512x512 text-to-image in just 1-4 steps. The reference small-and-fast diffusion model: faster than the Hyper variants, broadly capable, no fine-tune bias.
models.description: Stability AI's Adversarial Diffusion Distillation (ADD) of Stable Diffusion 2.1. Single CLIP-H text encoder (1024-dim), 512×512 latent decode, designed for 1-step inference with 4 steps as the quality sweet spot. Pick over the SD 1.5 Hyper variants when you want the canonical Stability baseline rather than a fine-tune; pick over SDXL Turbo when 512×512 is enough and you want roughly half the VRAM footprint. Subject to the Stability AI Community License — free for individuals and commercial use below $1M annual revenue.
attributions: Stability AI — SD Turbo (Sauer, Lorenz, Blattmann, Rombach, 2023). ADD distillation of Stable Diffusion 2.1.

models.summary: Stability AI's distilled SDXL — 512×512 text-to-image in just 1-4 steps with SDXL's stronger prompt adherence and aesthetic baseline. Heavier than SD Turbo; lighter than full SDXL.
models.description: Stability AI's Adversarial Diffusion Distillation (ADD) of SDXL base. Dual text encoders (CLIP-L 768 + OpenCLIP-G 1280, combined to 2048), 512×512 latent decode (not the 1024 of full SDXL), 1-4 step distillation. Pick over SD Turbo when prompt adherence + compositional quality matter more than throughput; pick over JuggernautXL Lightning when you want the Stability baseline rather than a community fine-tune, and 512×512 is enough output size. Subject to the Stability AI Community License — same $1M-revenue commercial threshold as SD Turbo.
attributions: Stability AI — SDXL Turbo (Sauer, Lorenz, Blattmann, Rombach, 2023). ADD distillation of Stable Diffusion XL.

models.summary: Small (1.5B-parameter) Apache-2.0 code-focused chat LLM from Alibaba. Fast to load and dispatch — the right pilot for validating SQL-defined LLM infrastructure.
models.description: Alibaba's 1.5B-param code-tuned Qwen 2.5 variant. ChatML chat template; reliable EOG discipline. Registered as a pair of SQL-defined models: qwen25_coder_1_5b_chat (ChatCompleter, owns the GGUF session) and qwen25_coder_1_5b (TextGenerator, delegates to the chat model — zero additional VRAM cost).

models.summary: Middle (3B-parameter) Apache-2.0 code-focused chat LLM from Alibaba. Meaningfully more coherent on multi-section HTML and short scripts than the 1.5B sibling.
models.description: Alibaba's 3B-param code-tuned Qwen 2.5 variant. ChatML chat template; reliable EOG discipline. Registered as a pair of SQL-defined models: qwen25_coder_3b_chat (ChatCompleter, owns the GGUF session) and qwen25_coder_3b (TextGenerator, delegates to the chat model — zero additional VRAM cost).

models.summary: Large (7B-parameter) Apache-2.0 code-focused chat LLM from Alibaba. Handles full single-file pages with embedded CSS/JS and multi-paragraph rationale.
models.description: Alibaba's 7B-param code-tuned Qwen 2.5 variant. Q5_K_M (not Q4) for higher fidelity. ChatML chat template; reliable EOG discipline. Registered as a pair of SQL-defined models: qwen25_coder_7b_chat (ChatCompleter, owns the GGUF session) and qwen25_coder_7b (TextGenerator, delegates to the chat model — zero additional VRAM cost). Default temperature 0.5 (vs 0.7 for smaller siblings) favours deterministic code output.

models.summary: Small (1.1B-parameter) Apache-2.0 chat LLM with a distinctly 2023-era voice. Useful for tonal A/B comparison against modern instruction-tuned models, and the cheapest LLM to dispatch in the zoo.
models.description: Community-trained 1.1B chat model on the Zephyr template. Tiny (~700 MB) and fast — pick when latency or footprint trump quality. Registered as a pair of SQL-defined models: tinyllama_1_1b_chat (ChatCompleter, owns the GGUF session) and tinyllama_1_1b (TextGenerator, delegates to the chat model — zero additional VRAM cost).

models.summary: IBM's small (1B total, 400M active) mixture-of-experts chat LLM. Apache-2.0. Distinct 'enterprise-y' instruction-tuning voice — frequently bullet-pointed, careful with caveats.
models.description: IBM's MoE-architecture small chat model. 1B total parameters, 400M active per token. Apache-2.0 fully unencumbered for commercial use. Registered as a pair of SQL-defined models: granite31_1b_chat (ChatCompleter, owns the GGUF session) and granite31_1b (TextGenerator, delegates to the chat model — zero additional VRAM cost).

models.summary: TII (UAE) trained 1B chat LLM. Custom Falcon LLM License 2.0 (broadly permissive with acceptable-use policy). Distinct vocabulary and style — useful for family-comparison tonal A/B.
models.description: Technology Innovation Institute's 1B-param chat-tuned model. ChatML chat template. Registered as a pair of SQL-defined models: falcon3_1b_chat (ChatCompleter, owns the GGUF session) and falcon3_1b (TextGenerator, delegates to the chat model — zero additional VRAM cost).

models.summary: Solid 7B-parameter Apache-2.0 chat model. The 'just give me a good general-purpose local LLM' default. Note: no native system role — prepend system text to the first user message if needed.
models.description: Apache-2.0 7B instruction-tuned LLM. Solid general-purpose default. Registered as a pair of SQL-defined models: mistral_7b_chat (ChatCompleter, owns the GGUF session) and mistral_7b (TextGenerator, delegates to the chat model — zero additional VRAM cost).

models.summary: Meta's 8B chat model. The zoo's most reliable chat discipline. Requires accepting Meta's Llama 3.1 Community License before download.
models.description: Meta's 8B-param instruction-tuned LLM. The most reliable chat discipline in the zoo — consistently emits `<|eot_id|>` on trivial prompts. Registered as a pair of SQL-defined models: llama31_8b_chat (ChatCompleter, owns the GGUF session) and llama31_8b (TextGenerator, delegates to the chat model — zero additional VRAM cost). Requires accepting the Llama 3.1 Community License before download.

models.summary: Emits a per-frame speech probability (one value every 32 ms at 16 kHz). Tiny (~2 MB). Pre-filter audio before transcribing it so the speech recognizer doesn't waste cycles on silence.
models.description: Tiny (~2.2 MB) voice-activity classifier. Returns a Float32 probability of speech per 32 ms frame at 16 kHz (≥0.5 conventionally = speech). Standard preprocessor in front of Whisper / STT pipelines — threshold the output to skip non-speech frames so the recognizer only sees audio that actually contains voice.

models.summary: Same voice-activity detector as Silero VAD, half the disk. Pick when bundle size matters (containers, serverless cold-start, edge).
models.description: Half-precision variant of Silero VAD — ~1.15 MB on disk (vs 2.24 MB for fp32). Same per-32 ms-frame speech-probability output at 16 kHz; pick this when deployment footprint matters (container images, serverless cold-start, edge bundles). On CPU runtimes that upcast fp16 → fp32 internally, expect identical latency to the fp32 variant; on GPU / NPU with native fp16, expect a modest speedup.

models.summary: Whisper at the next size up — better transcription accuracy, ~3× the cost of Base. Pick when quality matters more than throughput.
models.description: OpenAI Whisper, large size. Better accuracy than base, ~3x cost.
attributions: OpenAI — Whisper (Radford, Kim, Xu et al.)

models.summary: Whisper at the next size up — better transcription accuracy, ~3× the cost of Base. Pick when quality matters more than throughput.
models.description: OpenAI Whisper, small size. Better accuracy than base, ~3x cost.
attributions: OpenAI — Whisper (Radford, Kim, Xu et al.)

models.summary: Convert speech to text in 99 languages. Balanced speed / accuracy default — start here for transcribing audio recordings.
models.description: OpenAI Whisper, base size. Balanced speed/accuracy for English STT.
attributions: OpenAI — Whisper (Radford, Kim, Xu et al.)

models.summary: Smallest Whisper — fastest transcription, least accurate. Use on phones, embedded devices, or for ballpark transcription.
models.description: OpenAI Whisper, tiny size. Fastest but least accurate. Good for resource-constrained environments.
attributions: OpenAI — Whisper (Radford, Kim, Xu et al.)

models.summary: Embed images and text into the **same vector space** so you can search for images with words (or words with images). The original cross-modal model — the building block of zero-shot classification and CLIP-based search.
models.description: OpenAI CLIP for image-text contrastive embeddings. Use for zero-shot image classification, cross-modal search.
attributions: OpenAI — CLIP (Radford, Kim, Hallacy et al.)
